title: The Road
subtitle: Data lifecycle & discovery

hook.metaphor: Before you can drive safely, you need to know the road — every turn, every place the car and its cargo go. In DRC, the 'road' is your data's journey: where it's created, where it flows, where it rests, and where it should die.
hook.scenario: Your team is asked: "Where is all our customer data?" After two days of asking around, nobody can give a complete answer. That gap — not a hacker — is your first real risk.

learn: Data has a lifecycle: it is **created or collected**, **stored**, **used and shared**, and eventually **archived or deleted**. Risk and compliance obligations attach at every stage — not just at the moment of a breach.

You cannot govern, protect, or prove compliance for data you cannot see. So the foundational capability isn't a lock — it's a **map**. Knowing what data you hold, where it lives, how sensitive it is, and how it flows is the precondition for everything else.

Three building blocks make data visible: **discovery** (find data across systems), **classification** (label it — public, internal, PII, sensitive), and **lineage** (trace how it moves from source to destination).
buildBridge: So your product must be able to **discover data across heterogeneous systems, classify it by sensitivity, and trace its lineage** — because every downstream feature (risk scoring, controls, audit) depends on a trustworthy map of the data you hold.

interactions.prompt: Walk the data lifecycle. Click each stage to see the risk that lives there and the control that addresses it.

stages.label: Collect
stages.risk: Grabbing more than you need, or without a lawful basis / consent.
stages.control: Data minimization + consent capture at the point of collection.

stages.label: Store
stages.risk: Sensitive data sitting readable, in unknown places, accessible too widely.
stages.control: Discovery + classification, encryption at rest, least-privilege access.

stages.label: Use
stages.risk: Data used beyond its original purpose, or copied into shadow systems.
stages.control: Purpose limitation, masking/tokenization, lineage to trace copies.

stages.label: Share
stages.risk: Data leaving your boundary to third parties or across regions.
stages.control: Access controls, data-sharing agreements, residency rules.

stages.label: Delete
stages.risk: Keeping data forever — over-retention you can be asked to prove you ended.
stages.control: Retention limits + verifiable deletion across all copies.

interactions.prompt: Leadership wants a DRC product shipped fast. Which capability do you build *first*?

options.label: Encryption everywhere
options.feedback: Encryption matters — but encrypting data you haven't found or classified leaves blind spots. You'd be locking some doors in a house whose floor plan you don't have.

options.label: Data discovery & classification
options.feedback: Right. You can't protect, govern, or prove compliance for data you can't see. Discovery + classification is the map every other capability is built on.

options.label: A compliance reporting dashboard
options.feedback: Reports are only as honest as the data behind them. A dashboard built before discovery reports on a partial picture — confidently wrong.

interactions.prompt: Drag each item to its sensitivity label. (Click an item, then click a bucket.)

teachBack: In one line: *You can't govern data you can't see — so the first DRC capability is a living map of your data, not a lock.*

checkpoint.question: Why is data discovery the foundation of a DRC product?
options: Because regulators require a discovery tool by name
Because you can't protect, govern, or prove compliance for data you can't see
Because discovery is the cheapest feature to build
checkpoint.explanation: Every downstream capability — controls, risk scoring, audit — depends on knowing what data you actually hold.

checkpoint.question: Which trio makes data visible and governable?
checkpoint.explanation: Discovery finds it, classification labels its sensitivity, lineage traces how it flows.
